cards.name: High-Bar Back Squat
cards.cardSummary: High-Bar Back Squat is a barbell strength exercise for heavy bilateral force production with a more upright torso and high lower-body loading ceiling. Use it when the training goal is force production, tissue capacity, and repeatable positions—not conditioning volume.
cards.bestPlacement: Capacity phase after Prepare & Access, Movement Intelligence, and any true Output work. It can support explosiveness when coached with high intent and full rest, but it should not be programmed as a fatigue-based plyometric or conditioning station.
cards.description: Perform High-Bar Back Squat as a controlled, progressively loaded barbell strength pattern. The athlete creates tension before the first rep, moves through the range they can own, and finishes each repetition with the same posture and control they started with.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Squat / Knee-Dominant Strength. Score Strength high. Explosiveness can receive secondary credit only when the delivery uses crisp intent, appropriate load, and full recovery without changing this into Output or conditioning.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set your body first, move the bar with control, and make every rep look repeatable. Strong and sharp beats heavy and sloppy.
cards.whyItWorks: The barbell provides a scalable external resistance that forces the athlete to organize posture, brace, produce force, and control the loaded path. For High-Bar Back Squat, the main adaptation is strength: higher force capacity, tissue tolerance, and repeatable joint positions.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the primary adaptation is loaded force development. Place after high-skill or true power work so fatigue from heavy strength does not steal speed, jumping, or movement quality.
cards.commonMisuse: Do not chase load, fatigue, or bar speed after technique breaks. Do not tag or program this primarily as explosiveness unless the delivery is deliberately low-volume, high-intent, and fully recovered.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range of motion, tempo, stance, support, and rest before adding complexity. Progress only when posture, breathing, and bar path are repeatable.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Coach High-Bar Back Squat as a strength-focused barbell pattern with deliberate setup, controlled range, and clean reset between reps.
setup: Set the rack, bench, box, blocks, or bar height so the athlete can start and finish safely.
Load the bar conservatively for the athlete's current pattern quality.
Establish stance, grip, brace, and eye line before the first rep.
Confirm the athlete has a clear re-rack, set-down, or bail plan.
execution_steps: Brace before the bar moves.
Move through the athlete's controllable range without chasing extra depth or range.
Keep the bar path consistent and the working joints aligned.
Finish the rep under control, reset breath and brace, then repeat.
coach_cues: Stop before the rep gets ugly.
breathing_cues: Inhale and brace before the effort.
Exhale through the sticking point or reset at the top as appropriate.
Do not let breath-holding hide poor position.
common_faults: Adding load before the setup is repeatable.
Rushing the eccentric or losing position at end range.
Turning a strength lift into a conditioning set.
Grinding through pain, dizziness, or obvious compensation.
quality_gate: Athlete can repeat the same rep at the planned RPE.
Coach would be comfortable adding small load next week.
stop_signs: Pain appears or changes the pattern.
Bar speed slows into a technical grind.
Athlete loses brace, alignment, or safe rack/set-down control.
Dizziness, nausea, or panic breathing appears.

scaling.youth_beginner: Teach the pattern unloaded or with a PVC/light bar first. Use short sets, simple cues, and stop before fatigue changes alignment.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use an empty bar or light plates only when the athlete owns the setup, depth/range, and reset. Keep reps crisp and coach-supervised.
scaling.teen: Progress load gradually after movement standards are consistent. Prioritize repeatable positions over max attempts.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use conservative load, reduced range, or rack/bench support. Build confidence with 2-3 quality sets before adding volume.
scaling.adult_advanced: Load progressively within the prescribed RPE and use tempo, pause, or intent changes to match the training block without turning it into conditioning.
scaling.older_adult: Use reduced range, higher handles/blocks/box, slower tempo, and longer rest. Avoid maximal strain and prioritize joint comfort.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only with medical clearance and symptom-free bracing. Avoid breath-holding, excessive intra-abdominal pressure, and positions that cause heaviness, leaking, pain, or coning.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment. Scale by training age, body proportions, symptoms, recovery state, and sport demand rather than gender alone.
pairsWellAfter: Low-volume output or sprint/jump work when used in the same session
pairsWellBefore: Sustained capacity only if technique remains safe
doNotUseWhen: No safe equipment setup or supervision for the athlete's level.
Pain appears in the loaded pattern.
The session priority is fresh speed or jump output and the athlete is already fatigued.
The coach plans to use it as a burnout conditioning station.
mediaReferences: Internal Vortex barbell strength demo recommended
NSCA-style resistance training technique standards
mediaInternalNotes: Strength score: 5/5. Explosiveness delivery potential: 2/5; only score it when reps are crisp, rested, and intent-driven.
Film at least one side angle and one front/45-degree angle for bar path and alignment.

readiness_checks: Athlete can demonstrate the unloaded or lightly loaded pattern without pain.
Athlete can brace and breathe without dizziness or Valsalva dependence.
Athlete can stop the set before technique deteriorates.
contraindications: Painful knee flexion or unstable knee position under load.

cards.name: Barbell Split Squat

cards.athleteLanguage: Set your body first, move the bar with control, and make every rep look repeatable. Strong and sharp beats heavy and sloppy.
cards.whyItWorks: The barbell provides a scalable external resistance that forces the athlete to organize posture, brace, produce force, and control the loaded path. For Barbell Split Squat, the main adaptation is strength: higher force capacity, tissue tolerance, and repeatable joint positions.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the primary adaptation is loaded force development. Place after high-skill or true power work so fatigue from heavy strength does not steal speed, jumping, or movement quality.

cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range of motion, tempo, stance, support, and rest before adding complexity. Progress only when posture, breathing, and bar path are repeatable.

cards.name: Barbell Lateral Lunge
cards.cardSummary: Barbell Lateral Lunge is a barbell strength exercise for frontal-plane lower-body strength and adductor capacity. Use it when the training goal is force production, tissue capacity, and repeatable positions—not conditioning volume.
cards.bestPlacement: Capacity phase after Prepare & Access, Movement Intelligence, and any true Output work. It can support explosiveness when coached with high intent and full rest, but it should not be programmed as a fatigue-based plyometric or conditioning station.
cards.description: Perform Barbell Lateral Lunge as a controlled, progressively loaded barbell strength pattern. The athlete creates tension before the first rep, moves through the range they can own, and finishes each repetition with the same posture and control they started with.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Squat / Knee-Dominant Strength. Score Strength high. Explosiveness can receive secondary credit only when the delivery uses crisp intent, appropriate load, and full recovery without changing this into Output or conditioning.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set your body first, move the bar with control, and make every rep look repeatable. Strong and sharp beats heavy and sloppy.
cards.whyItWorks: The barbell provides a scalable external resistance that forces the athlete to organize posture, brace, produce force, and control the loaded path. For Barbell Lateral Lunge, the main adaptation is strength: higher force capacity, tissue tolerance, and repeatable joint positions.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the primary adaptation is loaded force development. Place after high-skill or true power work so fatigue from heavy strength does not steal speed, jumping, or movement quality.

cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range of motion, tempo, stance, support, and rest before adding complexity. Progress only when posture, breathing, and bar path are repeatable.

cards.name: Romanian Deadlift
cards.cardSummary: Capacity Romanian deadlift emphasizing controlled eccentric hip hinge, hamstring length under load, and posterior-chain strength through partial ROM deadlift pattern.
cards.bestPlacement: Capacity phase after Prepare & Access, Movement Intelligence, and any true Output work. It can support explosiveness when coached with high intent and full rest, but it should not be programmed as a fatigue-based plyometric or conditioning station.
cards.description: The athlete starts standing with load at hips, pushes hips back with soft knees, lowers load along the thighs to hamstring stretch, and returns by driving hips forward while maintaining neutral spine.
cards.coachLanguage: Use for hamstring and glute strength with intentional eccentric control and full rest. Capacity RDL is not a stiff-leg deadlift race or Fitness AMRAP.
cards.athleteLanguage: Soft knees, push your hips back, feel your hamstrings stretch, and drive your hips forward to stand.
cards.whyItWorks: The barbell provides a scalable external resistance that forces the athlete to organize posture, brace, produce force, and control the loaded path. For Romanian Deadlift, the main adaptation is strength: higher force capacity, tissue tolerance, and repeatable joint positions.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the primary adaptation is loaded force development. Place after high-skill or true power work so fatigue from heavy strength does not steal speed, jumping, or movement quality.

cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range of motion, tempo, stance, support, and rest before adding complexity. Progress only when posture, breathing, and bar path are repeatable.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Standing hinge with soft knees, load tracks close to legs, controlled eccentric to hamstring stretch, hip drive to stand with neutral spine.
setup: Select barbell, dumbbells, or kettlebells based on skill.
Start from hang position at hip height.
Feet hip-width, soft knees.
Confirm deadlift hinge competency first.
execution_steps: Brace trunk and set neutral spine.
Push hips back with soft knees.
Slide load down thighs — shins stay relatively vertical.
Stop at hamstring stretch without lumbar rounding.
Drive hips forward to stand tall.
Rest fully between sets.
breathing_cues: Inhale and brace before the effort.
Exhale through the sticking point or reset at the top as appropriate.
Do not let breath-holding hide poor position.
common_faults: Lumbar rounding to reach floor.
Bar drifting away from legs.
Bending knees into squat pattern.
Bouncing at bottom.
Rushing eccentric — no tempo control.
Prescribing before deadlift competency.
Adding load before the setup is repeatable.
Rushing the eccentric or losing position at end range.
Turning a strength lift into a conditioning set.
Grinding through pain, dizziness, or obvious compensation.

cards.name: Stiff-Leg Deadlift
cards.cardSummary: Stiff-Leg Deadlift is a barbell strength exercise for long-lever posterior-chain strength and hamstring tissue capacity. Use it when the training goal is force production, tissue capacity, and repeatable positions—not conditioning volume.
cards.bestPlacement: Capacity phase after Prepare & Access, Movement Intelligence, and any true Output work. It can support explosiveness when coached with high intent and full rest, but it should not be programmed as a fatigue-based plyometric or conditioning station.
cards.description: Perform Stiff-Leg Deadlift as a controlled, progressively loaded barbell strength pattern. The athlete creates tension before the first rep, moves through the range they can own, and finishes each repetition with the same posture and control they started with.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Hinge / Posterior-Chain Strength. Score Strength high. Explosiveness can receive secondary credit only when the delivery uses crisp intent, appropriate load, and full recovery without changing this into Output or conditioning.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set your body first, move the bar with control, and make every rep look repeatable. Strong and sharp beats heavy and sloppy.
cards.whyItWorks: The barbell provides a scalable external resistance that forces the athlete to organize posture, brace, produce force, and control the loaded path. For Stiff-Leg Deadlift, the main adaptation is strength: higher force capacity, tissue tolerance, and repeatable joint positions.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the primary adaptation is loaded force development. Place after high-skill or true power work so fatigue from heavy strength does not steal speed, jumping, or movement quality.
cards.commonMisuse: Do not chase load, fatigue, or bar speed after technique breaks. Do not tag or program this primarily as explosiveness unless the delivery is deliberately low-volume, high-intent, and fully recovered.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range of motion, tempo, stance, support, and rest before adding complexity. Progress only when posture, breathing, and bar path are repeatable.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Coach Stiff-Leg Deadlift as a strength-focused barbell pattern with deliberate setup, controlled range, and clean reset between reps.
setup: Set the rack, bench, box, blocks, or bar height so the athlete can start and finish safely.
Load the bar conservatively for the athlete's current pattern quality.
Establish stance, grip, brace, and eye line before the first rep.
Confirm the athlete has a clear re-rack, set-down, or bail plan.
execution_steps: Brace before the bar moves.
Move through the athlete's controllable range without chasing extra depth or range.
Keep the bar path consistent and the working joints aligned.
Finish the rep under control, reset breath and brace, then repeat.
coach_cues: Stop before the rep gets ugly.
breathing_cues: Inhale and brace before the effort.
Exhale through the sticking point or reset at the top as appropriate.
Do not let breath-holding hide poor position.
common_faults: Adding load before the setup is repeatable.
Rushing the eccentric or losing position at end range.
Turning a strength lift into a conditioning set.
Grinding through pain, dizziness, or obvious compensation.
quality_gate: Athlete can repeat the same rep at the planned RPE.
Coach would be comfortable adding small load next week.
stop_signs: Pain appears or changes the pattern.
Bar speed slows into a technical grind.
Athlete loses brace, alignment, or safe rack/set-down control.
Dizziness, nausea, or panic breathing appears.

scaling.youth_beginner: Teach the pattern unloaded or with a PVC/light bar first. Use short sets, simple cues, and stop before fatigue changes alignment.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use an empty bar or light plates only when the athlete owns the setup, depth/range, and reset. Keep reps crisp and coach-supervised.
scaling.teen: Progress load gradually after movement standards are consistent. Prioritize repeatable positions over max attempts.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use conservative load, reduced range, or rack/bench support. Build confidence with 2-3 quality sets before adding volume.
scaling.adult_advanced: Load progressively within the prescribed RPE and use tempo, pause, or intent changes to match the training block without turning it into conditioning.
scaling.older_adult: Use reduced range, higher handles/blocks/box, slower tempo, and longer rest. Avoid maximal strain and prioritize joint comfort.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only with medical clearance and symptom-free bracing. Avoid breath-holding, excessive intra-abdominal pressure, and positions that cause heaviness, leaking, pain, or coning.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment. Scale by training age, body proportions, symptoms, recovery state, and sport demand rather than gender alone.
pairsWellAfter: Low-volume output or sprint/jump work when used in the same session
pairsWellBefore: Sustained capacity only if technique remains safe
doNotUseWhen: No safe equipment setup or supervision for the athlete's level.
Pain appears in the loaded pattern.
The session priority is fresh speed or jump output and the athlete is already fatigued.
The coach plans to use it as a burnout conditioning station.
mediaReferences: Internal Vortex barbell strength demo recommended
NSCA-style resistance training technique standards
mediaInternalNotes: Strength score: 5/5. Explosiveness delivery potential: 1/5; only score it when reps are crisp, rested, and intent-driven.
Film at least one side angle and one front/45-degree angle for bar path and alignment.

readiness_checks: Athlete can demonstrate the unloaded or lightly loaded pattern without pain.
Athlete can brace and breathe without dizziness or Valsalva dependence.
Athlete can stop the set before technique deteriorates.
contraindications: Back pain, inability to brace, or loss of neutral trunk under load.

Load the bar conservatively for the athlete's current pattern quality.
Establish stance, grip, brace, and eye line before the first rep.
Confirm the athlete has a clear re-rack, set-down, or bail plan.
execution_steps: Brace before the bar moves.
Move through the athlete's controllable range without chasing extra depth or range.
Keep the bar path consistent and the working joints aligned.
Finish the rep under control, reset breath and brace, then repeat.
coach_cues: Stop before the rep gets ugly.
breathing_cues: Inhale and brace before the effort.
Exhale through the sticking point or reset at the top as appropriate.
Do not let breath-holding hide poor position.
common_faults: Adding load before the setup is repeatable.
Rushing the eccentric or losing position at end range.
Turning a strength lift into a conditioning set.
Grinding through pain, dizziness, or obvious compensation.
quality_gate: Athlete can repeat the same rep at the planned RPE.
Coach would be comfortable adding small load next week.
stop_signs: Pain appears or changes the pattern.
Bar speed slows into a technical grind.
Athlete loses brace, alignment, or safe rack/set-down control.
Dizziness, nausea, or panic breathing appears.

scaling.youth_beginner: Teach the pattern unloaded or with a PVC/light bar first. Use short sets, simple cues, and stop before fatigue changes alignment.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use an empty bar or light plates only when the athlete owns the setup, depth/range, and reset. Keep reps crisp and coach-supervised.
scaling.teen: Progress load gradually after movement standards are consistent. Prioritize repeatable positions over max attempts.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use conservative load, reduced range, or rack/bench support. Build confidence with 2-3 quality sets before adding volume.
scaling.adult_advanced: Load progressively within the prescribed RPE and use tempo, pause, or intent changes to match the training block without turning it into conditioning.
scaling.older_adult: Use reduced range, higher handles/blocks/box, slower tempo, and longer rest. Avoid maximal strain and prioritize joint comfort.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only with medical clearance and symptom-free bracing. Avoid breath-holding, excessive intra-abdominal pressure, and positions that cause heaviness, leaking, pain, or coning.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment. Scale by training age, body proportions, symptoms, recovery state, and sport demand rather than gender alone.
pairsWellAfter: Low-volume output or sprint/jump work when used in the same session
pairsWellBefore: Sustained capacity only if technique remains safe
doNotUseWhen: No safe equipment setup or supervision for the athlete's level.
Pain appears in the loaded pattern.
The session priority is fresh speed or jump output and the athlete is already fatigued.
The coach plans to use it as a burnout conditioning station.
mediaReferences: Internal Vortex barbell strength demo recommended
NSCA-style resistance training technique standards
mediaInternalNotes: Strength score: 5/5. Explosiveness delivery potential: 1/5; only score it when reps are crisp, rested, and intent-driven.
Film at least one side angle and one front/45-degree angle for bar path and alignment.

readiness_checks: Athlete can demonstrate the unloaded or lightly loaded pattern without pain.
Athlete can brace and breathe without dizziness or Valsalva dependence.
Athlete can stop the set before technique deteriorates.
contraindications: Back pain, inability to brace, or loss of neutral trunk under load.
Shoulder, wrist, or elbow pain in the required rack, press, or pull position.

cards.name: Bent-Over Barbell Row
cards.cardSummary: Bent-Over Barbell Row is a barbell strength exercise for upper-back and lats strength while holding a loaded hinge. Use it when the training goal is force production, tissue capacity, and repeatable positions—not conditioning volume.
cards.bestPlacement: Capacity phase after Prepare & Access, Movement Intelligence, and any true Output work. It can support explosiveness when coached with high intent and full rest, but it should not be programmed as a fatigue-based plyometric or conditioning station.
cards.description: Perform Bent-Over Barbell Row as a controlled, progressively loaded barbell strength pattern. The athlete creates tension before the first rep, moves through the range they can own, and finishes each repetition with the same posture and control they started with.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Pull / Hang / Grip Strength. Score Strength high. Explosiveness can receive secondary credit only when the delivery uses crisp intent, appropriate load, and full recovery without changing this into Output or conditioning.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set your body first, move the bar with control, and make every rep look repeatable. Strong and sharp beats heavy and sloppy.
cards.whyItWorks: The barbell provides a scalable external resistance that forces the athlete to organize posture, brace, produce force, and control the loaded path. For Bent-Over Barbell Row, the main adaptation is strength: higher force capacity, tissue tolerance, and repeatable joint positions.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the primary adaptation is loaded force development. Place after high-skill or true power work so fatigue from heavy strength does not steal speed, jumping, or movement quality.
cards.commonMisuse: Do not chase load, fatigue, or bar speed after technique breaks. Do not tag or program this primarily as explosiveness unless the delivery is deliberately low-volume, high-intent, and fully recovered.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range of motion, tempo, stance, support, and rest before adding complexity. Progress only when posture, breathing, and bar path are repeatable.

cards.cardSummary: Barbell T-Bar Row is a barbell strength exercise for heavy horizontal pulling strength with a constrained bar path. Use it when the training goal is force production, tissue capacity, and repeatable positions—not conditioning volume.
cards.bestPlacement: Capacity phase after Prepare & Access, Movement Intelligence, and any true Output work. It can support explosiveness when coached with high intent and full rest, but it should not be programmed as a fatigue-based plyometric or conditioning station.
cards.description: Perform Barbell T-Bar Row as a controlled, progressively loaded barbell strength pattern. The athlete creates tension before the first rep, moves through the range they can own, and finishes each repetition with the same posture and control they started with.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Pull / Hang / Grip Strength. Score Strength high. Explosiveness can receive secondary credit only when the delivery uses crisp intent, appropriate load, and full recovery without changing this into Output or conditioning.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set your body first, move the bar with control, and make every rep look repeatable. Strong and sharp beats heavy and sloppy.
cards.whyItWorks: The barbell provides a scalable external resistance that forces the athlete to organize posture, brace, produce force, and control the loaded path. For Barbell T-Bar Row, the main adaptation is strength: higher force capacity, tissue tolerance, and repeatable joint positions.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the primary adaptation is loaded force development. Place after high-skill or true power work so fatigue from heavy strength does not steal speed, jumping, or movement quality.
cards.commonMisuse: Do not chase load, fatigue, or bar speed after technique breaks. Do not tag or program this primarily as explosiveness unless the delivery is deliberately low-volume, high-intent, and fully recovered.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range of motion, tempo, stance, support, and rest before adding complexity. Progress only when posture, breathing, and bar path are repeatable.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Coach Barbell T-Bar Row as a strength-focused barbell pattern with deliberate setup, controlled range, and clean reset between reps.
setup: Set the rack, bench, box, blocks, or bar height so the athlete can start and finish safely.
Load the bar conservatively for the athlete's current pattern quality.
Establish stance, grip, brace, and eye line before the first rep.
Confirm the athlete has a clear re-rack, set-down, or bail plan.
execution_steps: Brace before the bar moves.
Move through the athlete's controllable range without chasing extra depth or range.
Keep the bar path consistent and the working joints aligned.
Finish the rep under control, reset breath and brace, then repeat.
coach_cues: Stop before the rep gets ugly.
breathing_cues: Inhale and brace before the effort.
Exhale through the sticking point or reset at the top as appropriate.
Do not let breath-holding hide poor position.
common_faults: Adding load before the setup is repeatable.
Rushing the eccentric or losing position at end range.
Turning a strength lift into a conditioning set.
Grinding through pain, dizziness, or obvious compensation.
quality_gate: Athlete can repeat the same rep at the planned RPE.
Coach would be comfortable adding small load next week.
stop_signs: Pain appears or changes the pattern.
Bar speed slows into a technical grind.
Athlete loses brace, alignment, or safe rack/set-down control.
Dizziness, nausea, or panic breathing appears.

scaling.youth_beginner: Teach the pattern unloaded or with a PVC/light bar first. Use short sets, simple cues, and stop before fatigue changes alignment.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use an empty bar or light plates only when the athlete owns the setup, depth/range, and reset. Keep reps crisp and coach-supervised.
scaling.teen: Progress load gradually after movement standards are consistent. Prioritize repeatable positions over max attempts.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use conservative load, reduced range, or rack/bench support. Build confidence with 2-3 quality sets before adding volume.
scaling.adult_advanced: Load progressively within the prescribed RPE and use tempo, pause, or intent changes to match the training block without turning it into conditioning.
scaling.older_adult: Use reduced range, higher handles/blocks/box, slower tempo, and longer rest. Avoid maximal strain and prioritize joint comfort.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only with medical clearance and symptom-free bracing. Avoid breath-holding, excessive intra-abdominal pressure, and positions that cause heaviness, leaking, pain, or coning.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment. Scale by training age, body proportions, symptoms, recovery state, and sport demand rather than gender alone.
pairsWellAfter: Low-volume output or sprint/jump work when used in the same session
pairsWellBefore: Sustained capacity only if technique remains safe
doNotUseWhen: No safe equipment setup or supervision for the athlete's level.
Pain appears in the loaded pattern.
The session priority is fresh speed or jump output and the athlete is already fatigued.
The coach plans to use it as a burnout conditioning station.

NSCA-style resistance training technique standards

Shoulder, wrist, or elbow pain in the required rack, press, or pull position.

cards.name: Zercher Carry
cards.cardSummary: Anterior-loaded carry held in the crooks of the elbows that builds trunk stiffness, upper-back strength, posture, and front-loaded bracing capacity.
cards.bestPlacement: Capacity phase after Prepare & Access, Movement Intelligence, and any true Output work. It can support explosiveness when coached with high intent and full rest, but it should not be programmed as a fatigue-based plyometric or conditioning station.
cards.description: The athlete holds a barbell, axle, or sandbag in the crooks of the elbows, braces, and walks while keeping the load close to the torso and posture tall.
cards.coachLanguage: Use as an advanced front-loaded carry after bear-hug and front-rack carry competency. Pad the bar if needed and keep the load close — moderate-to-high intensity with full rest.
cards.athleteLanguage: Hold the bar in your elbows, keep it close, brace your trunk, and walk tall.
cards.whyItWorks: The barbell provides a scalable external resistance that forces the athlete to organize posture, brace, produce force, and control the loaded path. For Zercher Carry, the main adaptation is strength: higher force capacity, tissue tolerance, and repeatable joint positions.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the primary adaptation is loaded force development. Place after high-skill or true power work so fatigue from heavy strength does not steal speed, jumping, or movement quality.

cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range of motion, tempo, stance, support, and rest before adding complexity. Progress only when posture, breathing, and bar path are repeatable.

cards.name: Front-Rack Carry
cards.cardSummary: Anterior-loaded carry that builds trunk stiffness, upper-back capacity, front-rack posture, and breathing under load.
cards.bestPlacement: Capacity phase after Prepare & Access, Movement Intelligence, and any true Output work. It can support explosiveness when coached with high intent and full rest, but it should not be programmed as a fatigue-based plyometric or conditioning station.
cards.description: The athlete holds one or two kettlebells or dumbbells in the front-rack position and walks while keeping the ribs down, elbows organized, and trunk stiff.
cards.coachLanguage: Use when the athlete needs front-loaded posture, core bracing, and upper-back strength. Great bridge to front squat and loaded carries — not a high-rep burnout walk.
cards.athleteLanguage: Hold the weights in the front rack, keep ribs down, elbows steady, and walk tall.
cards.whyItWorks: The barbell provides a scalable external resistance that forces the athlete to organize posture, brace, produce force, and control the loaded path. For Front-Rack Carry, the main adaptation is strength: higher force capacity, tissue tolerance, and repeatable joint positions.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the primary adaptation is loaded force development. Place after high-skill or true power work so fatigue from heavy strength does not steal speed, jumping, or movement quality.

cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range of motion, tempo, stance, support, and rest before adding complexity. Progress only when posture, breathing, and bar path are repeatable.

cards.name: Goblet Squat
cards.cardSummary: Foundational Capacity squat pattern using a front-loaded kettlebell or dumbbell to teach depth, trunk bracing, and progressive lower-body strength.
cards.bestPlacement: Use in the Capacity phase after Prepare/Access and any true Output work. Keep it before hard conditioning; pair with longer rest when strength or high-intent concentric speed is the goal.
cards.description: The athlete holds a kettlebell or dumbbell at the chest, sets feet shoulder-width, braces the trunk, and squats to controlled depth with knees tracking over toes.
cards.coachLanguage: Use as the foundational Capacity squat. Progressive overload with full rest — not a warm-up fluff set or circuit finisher. Add load only when every rep stays crisp.
cards.athleteLanguage: Hold the weight at your chest, brace your core, sit between your hips, and stand up strong.
cards.whyItWorks: The dumbbell load gives the athlete a scalable resistance problem while each limb, joint, and trunk position must organize independently. This develops usable force production without needing high impact or jump-based explosiveness.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the main adaptation is strength, force tolerance, and structural reserve. It can support later explosiveness when reps are coached with intent, but it should not replace true Output work.
cards.commonMisuse: The most common mistake is turning a strength lift into a rushed fatigue circuit or over-tagging it as explosiveness simply because the athlete moves hard. Keep load, tempo, rest, and technique aligned with strength first.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range of motion, stance support, tempo, set length, and rest. Progress only when posture, pain-free range, and repeatable rep quality are stable.

scoringNotes.explosiveness_condition: Positive only when delivered with crisp concentric intent, low fatigue, adequate rest, and no jump/plyometric substitution.
scoringNotes.not_explosiveness_when: High-rep fatigue, slow grinder reps, conditioning circuits, or sloppy tempo.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Hold kettlebell or dumbbell at chest, brace trunk, squat to controlled depth with knees tracking toes, and stand without losing posture.
setup: Select kettlebell or dumbbell load appropriate to skill.
Feet shoulder-width, toes slightly out.
Elbows down, weight close to chest.
Confirm pain-free range and supervision for youth.
execution_steps: Brace trunk and set neutral spine.
Sit hips down and back together.
Keep knees tracking over mid-foot.
Reach controlled depth without butt wink or collapse.
Drive through full foot to stand.
Rest fully before the next set.
breathing_cues: Big breath and brace at the top — exhale through the sticking point if needed without losing brace.
common_faults: Weight drifting away from chest.
Heels rising early.
Knees collapsing inward.
Excessive forward lean or lumbar flexion at bottom.
Rushing reps with short rest.
Using weight beyond clean rep capacity.
Chasing load before position is repeatable.
Holding the breath for the entire set instead of bracing and breathing.
Rushing the eccentric or bouncing out of the bottom.
Knees collapse inward or heels lose pressure.
quality_gate: Final rep looks close to the first rep.
stop_signs: Pain appears or increases during the set.
Technique changes more than one rep in a row.
Breathing becomes dizziness, bracing panic, or uncontrolled breath holding.
Grip, shoulder, spine, hip, knee, or ankle position becomes unsafe.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use very light dumbbells or bodyweight patterning first. Keep sets short, coach positions, and avoid max effort or fatigue races.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use moderate dumbbells for 2-3 sets with clean tempo. Progress load only after the athlete controls range and breathing.
scaling.teen: Use progressive load with technique guardrails. Keep 1-3 reps in reserve and avoid turning strength work into conditioning.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with conservative load, shorter range if needed, and stable stance/support. Build consistency before chasing heavier numbers.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress load, unilateral demand, pauses, tempo, or density while preserving strength intent and adequate rest.
scaling.older_adult: Use lighter load, slower tempo, stable support, and longer rest. Avoid breath-holding and reduce range if joints are symptomatic.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use clinician guidance when appropriate. Avoid breath-holding, heavy bracing, symptoms, and positions that create pelvic-floor or abdominal pressure concerns.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment. Scale by training age, limb length, symptoms, recovery, coordination, and available load rather than gender.
doNotUseWhen: Pain appears in the loaded pattern.
Athlete cannot control the dumbbells safely.
Grip or posture fails before the target tissues are trained.
The session needs true max-speed or jump Output and this would create fatigue first.
Knee pain with loaded squat
Cannot control knee valgus
Using as conditioning circuit
Youth max load without supervision

NSCA-style resistance training exercise technique standards

Keep Strength as the primary tenet.
Explosiveness score is optional and delivery-dependent; do not program as plyometrics.
Validator should confirm bilateral squat competency before front squat or RFESS.

Athlete can brace and breathe without dizziness.
Athlete can stop the set before technique deteriorates.
contraindications: Painful joint loading in the pattern.
Uncontrolled spine, shoulder, hip, knee, ankle, or wrist position.
No safe space or support for the variation.

phaseProfile.notes: Foundational Capacity squat. Master before front squat and heavy unilateral progressions. Supervision: recommended.

avoidBefore: Max velocity sprints in same session if heavy squat block precedes Output
Front squat if goblet depth is poor

cards.name: Double Dumbbell Front Squat
cards.cardSummary: Two-dumbbell front-rack squat that increases loading potential and trunk demand. Strength is the primary score; explosiveness is delivery-dependent, not the main intent.
cards.bestPlacement: Use in the Capacity phase after Prepare/Access and any true Output work. Keep it before hard conditioning; pair with longer rest when strength or high-intent concentric speed is the goal.
cards.description: Double Dumbbell Front Squat is a dumbbell-loaded strength exercise for lower-body force production. Load, range, tempo, and rest should support force production rather than metabolic fatigue.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Squat / knee-dominant strength. Score Strength high. Add a small Explosiveness score only when the coach uses crisp concentric intent, low fatigue, and adequate rest; do not treat it as a jump, plyometric, or conditioning station.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set position first, move the dumbbell with control, then finish every rep like you could repeat it cleanly.
cards.whyItWorks: The dumbbell load gives the athlete a scalable resistance problem while each limb, joint, and trunk position must organize independently. This develops usable force production without needing high impact or jump-based explosiveness.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the main adaptation is strength, force tolerance, and structural reserve. It can support later explosiveness when reps are coached with intent, but it should not replace true Output work.
cards.commonMisuse: The most common mistake is turning a strength lift into a rushed fatigue circuit or over-tagging it as explosiveness simply because the athlete moves hard. Keep load, tempo, rest, and technique aligned with strength first.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range of motion, stance support, tempo, set length, and rest. Progress only when posture, pain-free range, and repeatable rep quality are stable.

scoringNotes.explosiveness_condition: Positive only when delivered with crisp concentric intent, low fatigue, adequate rest, and no jump/plyometric substitution.
scoringNotes.not_explosiveness_when: High-rep fatigue, slow grinder reps, conditioning circuits, or sloppy tempo.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Double Dumbbell Front Squat: Two-dumbbell front-rack squat that increases loading potential and trunk demand. Use load that lets the athlete control the entire rep and finish with the same alignment they started with.
setup: Select dumbbell load that allows full control for Double Dumbbell Front Squat.
Set feet and stance before lifting the weight into position.
Brace ribs over pelvis and keep eyes forward.
Own the start position before the first rep.
execution_steps: Lower with control through the usable range while keeping knees tracking over the feet.
Drive the floor away and stand tall without bouncing or losing trunk position.
Reset breathing and tension between reps when load is challenging.
Stop the set when depth, alignment, or bracing changes.
coach_cues: Own the setup before the rep.
Control down; drive up without losing shape.
Stop before strength turns into survival.
athlete_cues: Move the weight, not your joints.
Leave one clean rep in reserve.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the lowering or reset.
Brace through the hard portion.
Exhale without collapsing posture.
common_faults: Chasing load before position is repeatable.
Holding the breath for the entire set instead of bracing and breathing.
Rushing the eccentric or bouncing out of the bottom.
Knees collapse inward or heels lose pressure.
quality_gate: Final rep looks close to the first rep.
stop_signs: Pain appears or increases during the set.
Technique changes more than one rep in a row.
Breathing becomes dizziness, bracing panic, or uncontrolled breath holding.
Grip, shoulder, spine, hip, knee, or ankle position becomes unsafe.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use very light dumbbells or bodyweight patterning first. Keep sets short, coach positions, and avoid max effort or fatigue races.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use moderate dumbbells for 2-3 sets with clean tempo. Progress load only after the athlete controls range and breathing.
scaling.teen: Use progressive load with technique guardrails. Keep 1-3 reps in reserve and avoid turning strength work into conditioning.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with conservative load, shorter range if needed, and stable stance/support. Build consistency before chasing heavier numbers.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress load, unilateral demand, pauses, tempo, or density while preserving strength intent and adequate rest.
scaling.older_adult: Use lighter load, slower tempo, stable support, and longer rest. Avoid breath-holding and reduce range if joints are symptomatic.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use clinician guidance when appropriate. Avoid breath-holding, heavy bracing, symptoms, and positions that create pelvic-floor or abdominal pressure concerns.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment. Scale by training age, limb length, symptoms, recovery, coordination, and available load rather than gender.

setup: Select dumbbell load that allows full control for Dumbbell Bulgarian Split Squat.
Set feet and stance before lifting the weight into position.
Brace ribs over pelvis and keep eyes forward.
Own the start position before the first rep.
execution_steps: Lower with control through the usable range while keeping knees tracking over the feet.
Drive the floor away and stand tall without bouncing or losing trunk position.
Reset breathing and tension between reps when load is challenging.
Stop the set when depth, alignment, or bracing changes.
coach_cues: Own the setup before the rep.
Control down; drive up without losing shape.
Stop before strength turns into survival.
athlete_cues: Move the weight, not your joints.
Leave one clean rep in reserve.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the lowering or reset.
Brace through the hard portion.
Exhale without collapsing posture.
common_faults: Chasing load before position is repeatable.
Holding the breath for the entire set instead of bracing and breathing.
Rushing the eccentric or bouncing out of the bottom.
Knees collapse inward or heels lose pressure.
quality_gate: Final rep looks close to the first rep.
stop_signs: Pain appears or increases during the set.
Technique changes more than one rep in a row.
Breathing becomes dizziness, bracing panic, or uncontrolled breath holding.
Grip, shoulder, spine, hip, knee, or ankle position becomes unsafe.

cards.name: Dumbbell Reverse Lunge
cards.cardSummary: Backward-stepping lunge that develops unilateral strength with lower forward knee shear than many forward-lunge variations. Strength is the primary score; explosiveness is delivery-dependent, not the main intent.
cards.bestPlacement: Use in the Capacity phase after Prepare/Access and any true Output work. Keep it before hard conditioning; pair with longer rest when strength or high-intent concentric speed is the goal.
cards.description: Dumbbell Reverse Lunge is a dumbbell-loaded strength exercise for lower-body force production. Load, range, tempo, and rest should support force production rather than metabolic fatigue.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Squat / knee-dominant strength. Score Strength high. Add a small Explosiveness score only when the coach uses crisp concentric intent, low fatigue, and adequate rest; do not treat it as a jump, plyometric, or conditioning station.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set position first, move the dumbbell with control, then finish every rep like you could repeat it cleanly.
cards.whyItWorks: The dumbbell load gives the athlete a scalable resistance problem while each limb, joint, and trunk position must organize independently. This develops usable force production without needing high impact or jump-based explosiveness.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the main adaptation is strength, force tolerance, and structural reserve. It can support later explosiveness when reps are coached with intent, but it should not replace true Output work.
cards.commonMisuse: The most common mistake is turning a strength lift into a rushed fatigue circuit or over-tagging it as explosiveness simply because the athlete moves hard. Keep load, tempo, rest, and technique aligned with strength first.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range of motion, stance support, tempo, set length, and rest. Progress only when posture, pain-free range, and repeatable rep quality are stable.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift: Loaded hip hinge that builds posterior-chain strength through controlled eccentric and strong hip extension. Use load that lets the athlete control the entire rep and finish with the same alignment they started with.
setup: Select dumbbell load that allows a clean hip hinge for Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift.
Set feet, grip the dumbbells, and soften the knees.
Brace the trunk and keep the dumbbells close to the body line.
Start with lats engaged and spine neutral.
execution_steps: Push the hips back while maintaining a neutral spine and close dumbbell path.
Lower until hamstring tension or position quality limits range.
Drive the hips through to stand without overextending the low back.
Keep each rep controlled enough that the next rep can be repeated cleanly.

Keep breathing controlled and joints stacked.
Begin only when the athlete can hold the starting shape without symptoms.
execution_steps: Move through the prescribed range with slow control.
Maintain brace, joint stack, and steady breathing.
Return to the start position without swinging.
Stop when control or alignment breaks.
coach_cues: Own the setup before the rep.
Control down; drive up without losing shape.
Stop before strength turns into survival.
athlete_cues: Move the weight, not your joints.
Leave one clean rep in reserve.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the lowering or reset.
Brace through the hard portion.
Exhale without collapsing posture.
common_faults: Chasing load before position is repeatable.
Holding the breath for the entire set instead of bracing and breathing.
Rushing the eccentric or bouncing out of the bottom.
Range increases after control has already been lost.
quality_gate: Final rep looks close to the first rep.
stop_signs: Pain appears or increases during the set.
Technique changes more than one rep in a row.
Breathing becomes dizziness, bracing panic, or uncontrolled breath holding.
Grip, shoulder, spine, hip, knee, or ankle position becomes unsafe.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use very light dumbbells or bodyweight patterning first. Keep sets short, coach positions, and avoid max effort or fatigue races.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use moderate dumbbells for 2-3 sets with clean tempo. Progress load only after the athlete controls range and breathing.
scaling.teen: Use progressive load with technique guardrails. Keep 1-3 reps in reserve and avoid turning strength work into conditioning.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with conservative load, shorter range if needed, and stable stance/support. Build consistency before chasing heavier numbers.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress load, unilateral demand, pauses, tempo, or density while preserving strength intent and adequate rest.
scaling.older_adult: Use lighter load, slower tempo, stable support, and longer rest. Avoid breath-holding and reduce range if joints are symptomatic.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use clinician guidance when appropriate. Avoid breath-holding, heavy bracing, symptoms, and positions that create pelvic-floor or abdominal pressure concerns.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment. Scale by training age, limb length, symptoms, recovery, coordination, and available load rather than gender.
pairsWellAfter: Mobility access for the target joints
pairsWellBefore: Conditioning placed later if movement quality remains high
doNotUseWhen: Pain appears in the loaded pattern.
Athlete cannot control the dumbbells safely.
Grip or posture fails before the target tissues are trained.
The session needs true max-speed or jump Output and this would create fatigue first.
mediaReferences: Internal Vortex dumbbell strength demo recommended
NSCA-style resistance training exercise technique standards
Vortex Exercise Card Authoring Guide: Capacity / strength sequencing rules
mediaInternalNotes: Film Standing Dumbbell Calf Raise from front/side where useful.
Keep Strength as the primary tenet.
Explosiveness score is optional and delivery-dependent; do not program as plyometrics.

readiness_checks: Athlete can demonstrate the unloaded pattern without pain.
Athlete can brace and breathe without dizziness.
Athlete can stop the set before technique deteriorates.
contraindications: Painful joint loading in the pattern.
Uncontrolled spine, shoulder, hip, knee, ankle, or wrist position.
No safe space or support for the variation.

cards.name: Seated Dumbbell Calf Raise
cards.cardSummary: Seated lower-leg strength exercise biased toward the soleus and ankle tissue capacity. Strength is the primary score; explosiveness is delivery-dependent, not the main intent.
cards.bestPlacement: Use in the Capacity phase after Prepare/Access and any true Output work. Keep it before hard conditioning; pair with longer rest when strength or high-intent concentric speed is the goal.
cards.description: Seated Dumbbell Calf Raise is a dumbbell-loaded strength exercise for trunk stiffness under load. Load, range, tempo, and rest should support force production rather than metabolic fatigue.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Tissue capacity accessory strength. Score Strength high. Add a small Explosiveness score only when the coach uses crisp concentric intent, low fatigue, and adequate rest; do not treat it as a jump, plyometric, or conditioning station.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set position first, move the dumbbell with control, then finish every rep like you could repeat it cleanly.
cards.whyItWorks: The dumbbell load gives the athlete a scalable resistance problem while each limb, joint, and trunk position must organize independently. This develops usable force production without needing high impact or jump-based explosiveness.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the main adaptation is strength, force tolerance, and structural reserve. It can support later explosiveness when reps are coached with intent, but it should not replace true Output work.
cards.commonMisuse: The most common mistake is turning a strength lift into a rushed fatigue circuit or over-tagging it as explosiveness simply because the athlete moves hard. Keep load, tempo, rest, and technique aligned with strength first.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range of motion, stance support, tempo, set length, and rest. Progress only when posture, pain-free range, and repeatable rep quality are stable.

scoringNotes.explosiveness_condition: Positive only when delivered with crisp concentric intent, low fatigue, adequate rest, and no jump/plyometric substitution.
scoringNotes.not_explosiveness_when: High-rep fatigue, slow grinder reps, conditioning circuits, or sloppy tempo.

Set the shoulder blades and grip before the first rep.
Brace ribs over pelvis; avoid flaring to create false range.
Begin from a stable floor, bench, kneeling, seated, or standing setup as the variation requires.
execution_steps: Press the dumbbells through the prescribed path without shrugging or rib flare.

cards.coachLanguage: Use for loaded horizontal push with intentional load and full rest. Supervise bench setup — not a max-effort powerlifting bench or Fitness circuit with short rest.

cards.name: One-Arm Dumbbell Row
cards.cardSummary: Unilateral dumbbell row that builds lat, upper-back, grip, trunk, and shoulder-control capacity.
cards.bestPlacement: Use in the Capacity phase after Prepare/Access and any true Output work. Keep it before hard conditioning; pair with longer rest when strength or high-intent concentric speed is the goal.
cards.description: The athlete supports one hand and knee on a bench, holds a dumbbell in the opposite hand with flat braced back, pulls elbow toward hip or ribs without torso rotation, squeezes at top, and lowers with control.
cards.coachLanguage: Use for single-arm pulling strength, left-right balance, and upper-back capacity without requiring hanging strength. Full rest between sets — not alternating-arm conditioning.
cards.athleteLanguage: Support your body, keep your back flat, pull your elbow toward your hip, and lower the weight with control.
cards.whyItWorks: The dumbbell load gives the athlete a scalable resistance problem while each limb, joint, and trunk position must organize independently. This develops usable force production without needing high impact or jump-based explosiveness.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the main adaptation is strength, force tolerance, and structural reserve. It can support later explosiveness when reps are coached with intent, but it should not replace true Output work.
cards.commonMisuse: The most common mistake is turning a strength lift into a rushed fatigue circuit or over-tagging it as explosiveness simply because the athlete moves hard. Keep load, tempo, rest, and technique aligned with strength first.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range of motion, stance support, tempo, set length, and rest. Progress only when posture, pain-free range, and repeatable rep quality are stable.

scoringNotes.explosiveness_condition: Positive only when delivered with crisp concentric intent, low fatigue, adequate rest, and no jump/plyometric substitution.
scoringNotes.not_explosiveness_when: High-rep fatigue, slow grinder reps, conditioning circuits, or sloppy tempo.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Bench-supported single-arm row: flat braced back, pull elbow toward hip without trunk rotation, control eccentric.

cards.description: Dumbbell Rear-Delt Row is a dumbbell-loaded strength exercise for upper-body pulling force. Load, range, tempo, and rest should support force production rather than metabolic fatigue.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Pull / hang / grip strength. Score Strength high. Add a small Explosiveness score only when the coach uses crisp concentric intent, low fatigue, and adequate rest; do not treat it as a jump, plyometric, or conditioning station.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set position first, move the dumbbell with control, then finish every rep like you could repeat it cleanly.
cards.whyItWorks: The dumbbell load gives the athlete a scalable resistance problem while each limb, joint, and trunk position must organize independently. This develops usable force production without needing high impact or jump-based explosiveness.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the main adaptation is strength, force tolerance, and structural reserve. It can support later explosiveness when reps are coached with intent, but it should not replace true Output work.
cards.commonMisuse: The most common mistake is turning a strength lift into a rushed fatigue circuit or over-tagging it as explosiveness simply because the athlete moves hard. Keep load, tempo, rest, and technique aligned with strength first.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range of motion, stance support, tempo, set length, and rest. Progress only when posture, pain-free range, and repeatable rep quality are stable.

scoringNotes.explosiveness_condition: Positive only when delivered with crisp concentric intent, low fatigue, adequate rest, and no jump/plyometric substitution.
scoringNotes.not_explosiveness_when: High-rep fatigue, slow grinder reps, conditioning circuits, or sloppy tempo.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Dumbbell Rear-Delt Row: Upper-back accessory row that strengthens rear delts and scapular control for pulling and pressing balance. Use load that lets the athlete control the entire rep and finish with the same alignment they started with.
setup: Select dumbbells that allow full-range pulling strength for Dumbbell Rear-Delt Row.
Set the trunk and shoulder position before initiating the pull.
Keep the wrist stacked and the neck relaxed.
Use support or hinge position as required by the variation.
execution_steps: Initiate the pull from the back, not the neck or low back.
Pull the dumbbells through the target path with elbow and scapular control.
Lower under control until the working tissues reload.
Stop when the athlete can no longer feel the target tissues without compensation.
coach_cues: Own the setup before the rep.
Control down; drive up without losing shape.
Stop before strength turns into survival.
athlete_cues: Move the weight, not your joints.
Leave one clean rep in reserve.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the lowering or reset.
Brace through the hard portion.
Exhale without collapsing posture.
common_faults: Chasing load before position is repeatable.
Holding the breath for the entire set instead of bracing and breathing.
Rushing the eccentric or bouncing out of the bottom.
Neck tension or low-back motion substitutes for upper-back pull.
quality_gate: Final rep looks close to the first rep.
stop_signs: Pain appears or increases during the set.
Technique changes more than one rep in a row.
Breathing becomes dizziness, bracing panic, or uncontrolled breath holding.
Grip, shoulder, spine, hip, knee, or ankle position becomes unsafe.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use very light dumbbells or bodyweight patterning first. Keep sets short, coach positions, and avoid max effort or fatigue races.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use moderate dumbbells for 2-3 sets with clean tempo. Progress load only after the athlete controls range and breathing.
scaling.teen: Use progressive load with technique guardrails. Keep 1-3 reps in reserve and avoid turning strength work into conditioning.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with conservative load, shorter range if needed, and stable stance/support. Build consistency before chasing heavier numbers.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress load, unilateral demand, pauses, tempo, or density while preserving strength intent and adequate rest.
scaling.older_adult: Use lighter load, slower tempo, stable support, and longer rest. Avoid breath-holding and reduce range if joints are symptomatic.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use clinician guidance when appropriate. Avoid breath-holding, heavy bracing, symptoms, and positions that create pelvic-floor or abdominal pressure concerns.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment. Scale by training age, limb length, symptoms, recovery, coordination, and available load rather than gender.
pairsWellAfter: Mobility access for the target joints
pairsWellBefore: Conditioning placed later if movement quality remains high
doNotUseWhen: Pain appears in the loaded pattern.
Athlete cannot control the dumbbells safely.
Grip or posture fails before the target tissues are trained.
The session needs true max-speed or jump Output and this would create fatigue first.

NSCA-style resistance training exercise technique standards

No safe space or support for the variation.

setup: Select dumbbells that challenge posture without forcing leaning for Dumbbell Farmer Carry.
Stand tall, set ribs over pelvis, and grip the handles firmly.
Create a quiet foot tripod before stepping.
Clear the walking lane before beginning.
execution_steps: Walk with quiet, controlled steps while maintaining tall posture.
Keep the load from pulling the ribs, shoulders, or pelvis out of position.
Turn or set down with control rather than swinging the dumbbells.
End the set before grip or posture fails.

cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Carry / trunk loaded bracing strength. Score Strength high. Add a small Explosiveness score only when the coach uses crisp concentric intent, low fatigue, and adequate rest; do not treat it as a jump, plyometric, or conditioning station.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set position first, move the dumbbell with control, then finish every rep like you could repeat it cleanly.
cards.whyItWorks: The dumbbell load gives the athlete a scalable resistance problem while each limb, joint, and trunk position must organize independently. This develops usable force production without needing high impact or jump-based explosiveness.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the main adaptation is strength, force tolerance, and structural reserve. It can support later explosiveness when reps are coached with intent, but it should not replace true Output work.
cards.commonMisuse: The most common mistake is turning a strength lift into a rushed fatigue circuit or over-tagging it as explosiveness simply because the athlete moves hard. Keep load, tempo, rest, and technique aligned with strength first.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by load, range of motion, stance support, tempo, set length, and rest. Progress only when posture, pain-free range, and repeatable rep quality are stable.

scoringNotes.explosiveness_condition: Positive only when delivered with crisp concentric intent, low fatigue, adequate rest, and no jump/plyometric substitution.
scoringNotes.not_explosiveness_when: High-rep fatigue, slow grinder reps, conditioning circuits, or sloppy tempo.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Dumbbell Plank Pull-Through: High-plank anti-rotation drill where the athlete drags a dumbbell across the body without shifting. Use load that lets the athlete control the entire rep and finish with the same alignment they started with.
setup: Select a dumbbell load that allows complete control for Dumbbell Plank Pull-Through.
Set the start position and brace before moving.
Keep breathing controlled and joints stacked.
Begin only when the athlete can hold the starting shape without symptoms.
execution_steps: Move through the prescribed range with slow control.
Maintain brace, joint stack, and steady breathing.
Return to the start position without swinging.
Stop when control or alignment breaks.
coach_cues: Own the setup before the rep.
Control down; drive up without losing shape.
Stop before strength turns into survival.
athlete_cues: Move the weight, not your joints.
Leave one clean rep in reserve.
breathing_cues: Inhale before the lowering or reset.
Brace through the hard portion.
Exhale without collapsing posture.
common_faults: Chasing load before position is repeatable.
Holding the breath for the entire set instead of bracing and breathing.
Rushing the eccentric or bouncing out of the bottom.
Range increases after control has already been lost.
quality_gate: Final rep looks close to the first rep.
stop_signs: Pain appears or increases during the set.
Technique changes more than one rep in a row.
Breathing becomes dizziness, bracing panic, or uncontrolled breath holding.
Grip, shoulder, spine, hip, knee, or ankle position becomes unsafe.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use very light dumbbells or bodyweight patterning first. Keep sets short, coach positions, and avoid max effort or fatigue races.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use moderate dumbbells for 2-3 sets with clean tempo. Progress load only after the athlete controls range and breathing.
scaling.teen: Use progressive load with technique guardrails. Keep 1-3 reps in reserve and avoid turning strength work into conditioning.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with conservative load, shorter range if needed, and stable stance/support. Build consistency before chasing heavier numbers.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress load, unilateral demand, pauses, tempo, or density while preserving strength intent and adequate rest.
scaling.older_adult: Use lighter load, slower tempo, stable support, and longer rest. Avoid breath-holding and reduce range if joints are symptomatic.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use clinician guidance when appropriate. Avoid breath-holding, heavy bracing, symptoms, and positions that create pelvic-floor or abdominal pressure concerns.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment. Scale by training age, limb length, symptoms, recovery, coordination, and available load rather than gender.
pairsWellAfter: Mobility access for the target joints
pairsWellBefore: Conditioning placed later if movement quality remains high
doNotUseWhen: Pain appears in the loaded pattern.
Athlete cannot control the dumbbells safely.
Grip or posture fails before the target tissues are trained.
The session needs true max-speed or jump Output and this would create fatigue first.
mediaReferences: Internal Vortex dumbbell strength demo recommended
NSCA-style resistance training exercise technique standards
Vortex Exercise Card Authoring Guide: Capacity / strength sequencing rules
mediaInternalNotes: Film Dumbbell Plank Pull-Through from front/side where useful.
Keep Strength as the primary tenet.
Explosiveness score is optional and delivery-dependent; do not program as plyometrics.

readiness_checks: Athlete can demonstrate the unloaded pattern without pain.
Athlete can brace and breathe without dizziness.
Athlete can stop the set before technique deteriorates.
contraindications: Painful joint loading in the pattern.
Uncontrolled spine, shoulder, hip, knee, ankle, or wrist position.
No safe space or support for the variation.
